prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
You’re probably wrong if you believe your memory is an accurate, objective record of what actually happened.

Why: memory is reconstructive, not like a camera. When you recall an event you patch together fragments, filling gaps with guesses, expectations, emotions and later information. That’s why eyewitness testimony is unreliable, why details shift after retelling, and why we confidently remember things that didn’t occur the way we think.

Quick examples: two people can vividly remember the same meeting but disagree on key details; hearing a different version of a story later can change your original memory; your mood at the time will bias what you recall.

What to do about it: treat your memories as fallible—take notes, keep timestamps or photos for important facts, ask others for corroboration, and be open to updating your recollection. That humility improves decisions and conversations.
